metrics.narrative: Brad Pittman articulated a clear business problem on the 13 Apr intro call: Welltower has never had center-led sourcing or procurement, and spend management lives fragmented across operating partners in a REIT structure with arm's-length constraints. He is building the function 'from the ground up' to aggregate portfolio-wide spend and contracts across operating partners, analogous to cross-portfolio sourcing in private equity. The pain is operational fragmentation and lost leverage; the opportunity is enterprise-wide spend visibility and contract compliance. However, NO quantified metrics were stated—no baseline spend leakage, no savings target, no cost-per-transaction, no cycle-time reduction goal. Brad has deep procurement transformation experience (Dollar Tree, PE portfolios, Coupa/Ariba/JAGGAER/Zip implementations) and described the imperative as strategic and transformative, but he did not quantify the business case on this early call. The gap: quantified impact (cost, savings, ROI, headcount leverage) remains to be developed. Next move: when Brad re-engages, co-develop a quantified value case using an ANA (spend analytics) pilot as offered in Next Step 03 May to 'document impact and hard savings.'

champion.narrative: Brad Pittman (VP Procurement) is a confirmed, actively developing champion. He sought out the 13 Apr intro call (not a cold outreach), engaged deeply for 30+ minutes, articulated detailed requirements, and explicitly validated Zycus's differentiation against competitors he knows well (Coupa, Ariba, JAGGAER, Zip, Scout RFP, Combinenet). He praised the unified platform architecture ('that's where ARIBA and Coupa got a bit lost… Scout RFP… sourcing piece had taken a step back') and confirmed fit ('this is right in your wheelhouse'). He requested the deck, invited future thought-leadership engagement, and stated he is 'a fan of pilots.' His engagement level was warm, collaborative, and substantive—he was learning, not just listening. However, his championship is DEVELOPING, not yet strong, because: (1) he is a team of one, building from scratch, with no internal coalition visible yet; (2) he has not introduced Zycus to the EB, CFO, legal, IT, or any other Welltower stakeholder (single-threaded); (3) he has not driven any action since the intro call (no pilot request, no demo ask, no follow-up, consistent with his stated 6-12 month internal review timeline); (4) he is not yet advocating internally or pushing Zycus into the evaluation process because the evaluation has not started. His strength: he is the functional buyer, the process owner, and the decision driver; he controls the timeline and the vendor engagement. His risk: he is new (3 months in), building credibility, and operating in a complex REIT structure where exec buy-in (CEO, CFO, CIO) will be required for a $300k+ platform decision. Current sentiment: positive and engaged. Current engagement: appropriately dormant (cooling per pulse, consistent with stated timeline). Next move: when Brad re-engages (expected Oct 2026–Apr 2027 per his 6-12 month window), convert interest into internal advocacy by offering a quantified pilot (ANA or sourcing event) that Brad can use to build his business case and engage the EB/CFO.

competition.narrative: Brad Pittman named six competitors or alternatives on the 13 Apr intro call, all from his prior implementations and evaluations, NOT from an active Welltower RFP or shortlist. He has hands-on experience with Coupa, SAP Ariba, JAGGAER, and Zip ('I've implemented Coupa and Ariba and Zip in the past') and used Scout RFP (Workday) and Combinenet (JAGGAER) in private-equity and transportation-sourcing contexts. He surfaced these as reference points to frame his requirements and validate Zycus's differentiation, and he explicitly criticized the bolt-on integration challenges of JAGGAER/Combinenet and Workday/Scout RFP ('those things get really hard to fold in… the sourcing piece had taken a step back… where ARIBA and Coupa got a bit lost'). His sentiment toward all six is NEGATIVE on integration and user experience, which is Zycus's core wedge (homogenous, unified S2P platform). However, Brad also stated Welltower is NOT in active evaluation ('probably a next year initiative'), so none of these competitors are live threats today—they are the KNOWN FIELD he will likely consider when he launches the evaluation in 6-12 months. He also mentioned 'an industry aggregator that gets utilized' as an incumbent or alternative procurement channel for operating partners, but did not name it. Threat assessment: Coupa and Ariba are the highest-profile enterprise S2P platforms and will almost certainly be on Brad's list when he evaluates; they are the default enterprise choices and carry the HIGHEST threat level once evaluation begins. JAGGAER, Zip, Scout RFP, and Combinenet are LOWER threats based on Brad's stated dissatisfaction and their narrower fit for Welltower's multi-entity complexity. The 'do nothing' or 'status quo' (fragmented, operating-partner-owned procurement with optional aggregator) is a MEDIUM threat because Brad is mandate-driven but resource-constrained (team of one) and the initiative is 6-12 months out—delay and internal prioritization could push this further. Zycus's wedge: Brad explicitly validated the unified platform, configurability, and Teams/Slack intake as differentiated and aligned with his requirements. How we win: move early with a quantified pilot (ANA or sourcing event) that Brad can use to build his business case and beat the status quo; position as the only true homogenous S2P platform that solves his multi-entity, multi-ERP, operating-partner complexity without bolt-on integration pain; activate the Merlin AI story (Brad showed strong interest in autonomous sourcing agent on the call: 'that could be really beneficial to you because… the agent can operate on behalf of you') to differentiate from Coupa/Ariba's older AI capabilities. Current competitive status: no active bake-off; Zycus is positioned early and positively; evaluation will trigger in 6-12 months and competitors will enter then.

identify_pain.narrative: Brad Pittman clearly articulated the pain on the 13 Apr intro call: Welltower has no center-led sourcing or procurement function, and spend management is fragmented across operating partners, each with their own systems, ERPs, and processes. The pain owner is Brad (VP Procurement, hired 3 months prior to transform the function). He described the problem as operational and strategic: 'They've never had a center led sourcing function. Today, sourcing procurement lives out with our operating partners… the actual procurement occurs in those operating partners' systems.' The consequence: no portfolio-wide spend visibility, no aggregated buying power, no contract compliance or preferred-supplier enforcement, and no leverage of Welltower's scale (analogous to private-equity cross-portfolio sourcing but with the added complexity of REIT arm's-length constraints and wholly-owned assets). Brad's mandate is to 'build it from the ground up,' aggregate spend, and create a unified procurement layer that allows operating partners to access centralized contracts and execute compliant transactions. The pain is confirmed and deeply felt (Brad left a stable, post-transformation role at Dollar Tree to take this greenfield challenge). The gap: pain is not yet quantified (no cost of fragmentation, no leakage %, no savings opportunity modeled). Pain is qualitative and strategic at this stage, appropriate for early discovery. Next move: quantify the pain through a spend-analytics pilot (ANA approach offered in Next Step 03 May) when Brad re-engages.

decision_criteria.narrative: Brad Pittman articulated detailed, specific decision criteria on the 13 Apr intro call, grounded in his prior implementations and Welltower's unique REIT/operating-partner structure. His requirements: (1) Source-to-Contract capabilities first (sourcing, contracting), with eProcurement as a critical transaction layer; (2) a unified platform allowing multiple operating partners—each with distinct ERPs, chart of accounts, business rules, and collaboration tools (Teams/Slack)—to access centralized Welltower contracts and execute compliant eProcurement transactions ('a single Welltower eProcurement application that would allow operating partners to come in and place procurement against sourced agreements'); (3) seamless integration with existing collaboration tools (Teams, Slack) for intake and orchestration to avoid adding new user interfaces ('why not start orchestration or intake where your organization already lies'); (4) an Amazon-like, easy-to-use shopping experience for end users to drive adoption across diverse operating partners; (5) configurability to handle complex, differentiated delegation-of-authority rules at the operating partner level without custom development ('configurability here would let you solve for what might be a little more complex delegation of authority'); (6) a homogenous, fully integrated platform (not bolt-on acquisitions) to avoid the data-silo and integration pain he experienced with the relevant stakeholder acquisition and Workday's Scout RFP bolt-on ('those things get really hard to fold in… the sourcing piece had taken a step back'). Brad explicitly rejected fragmented platforms and praised Zycus's unified architecture. These are confirmed, prospect-stated criteria with strong fit signaling. The gap: no formal RFP, RFI, or weighted scorecard yet; this was discovery, not a structured evaluation. Status: confirmed criteria from discovery conversation; formal scoring/validation ahead.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Brad Pittman (VP Procurement) showed strong, explicit interest in AI-powered autonomous sourcing on the 13 Apr intro call and is an ideal AI-hungry profile: resource-constrained (team of one), building a function from scratch, managing complex multi-entity procurement across operating partners, and looking for automation and leverage. Richard (Zycus) positioned Merlin autonomous sourcing agent as a fit for Brad's situation ('that could be really beneficial to you because… the agent can operate on behalf of you'), and Brad engaged positively. He also requested information and is open to pilots, signaling openness to innovation. However, AIS (AI excitement) fields in Salesforce are blank, so there is no quantified AI excitement score on record. The call content and Brad's profile strongly suggest AI Hungry tier: he has the pain (resource constraints, transformation mandate, need for leverage), the appetite (engaged with autonomous agent, asked for deck, open to pilots), and the sophistication (prior implementations of Coupa, Ariba, JAGGAER, Zip—he knows the technology landscape and is looking for differentiation). Tier assessment: AI Hungry (inferred from call and profile; not yet validated via AIS score or deeper AI-focused conversation). The AI positioning is under-activated (see ai_positioning_strength); Merlin should be the lead wedge when Brad re-engages.

deal_movement.summary: Deal created 29 Apr 2026 following a 13 Apr intro call between Richard Hunsinger (Zycus) and Brad Pittman (VP Procurement, Welltower). Brad is building Welltower's procurement function from the ground up and engaged Zycus in early discovery to understand S2P capabilities, multi-entity eProcurement support, and AI-powered intake. The call was substantive and warm; Brad validated Zycus's unified platform fit and requested the deck. Richard followed up 03 May with a Next Step update offering an ANA-first pilot approach to quantify savings and inviting Brad to the 13 May AI Virtual Summit. No buyer reply or follow-up since. Last Salesforce activity 03 May (rep-side Next Step update, 54 days ago). Last buyer engagement 13 Apr intro call (74 days ago). The deal is COOLING per the pulse but appropriately so: Brad explicitly stated the initiative is 'probably a next year' project with re-engagement 'likely within the next 6 to 12 months,' and he is reviewing Zycus internally. The movement is healthy early-stage discovery with a buyer-controlled long timeline; the cooling is not a stall but a planned dormancy consistent with Brad's stated cadence. No stage movement, no amount change, no close-date slip (date set at creation and not yet tested).

best_practice_check.summary: Three substantive win-strategy levers for this early-stage, high-fit, buyer-controlled deal: (1) Accelerate Brad's internal planning by offering a quantified, low-risk pilot (ANA spend analytics or sourcing event) that he can use to build his business case, secure EB/CFO buy-in, and move faster than the 6-12 month timeline—this beats status-quo inertia and locks Zycus preference before Coupa/Ariba engage. (2) Multi-thread beyond Brad (VP Procurement, team of one) to map and engage the EB/CFO sponsor, legal, IT/InfoSec, and FP&A stakeholders who will approve the $300k+ decision—Brad controls evaluation but cannot close alone; early exec access builds coalition and de-risks single-point-of-contact dependency. (3) Activate the Merlin autonomous sourcing agent narrative as the primary AI wedge—Brad showed explicit interest ('that could be really beneficial to you') and is resource-constrained; Merlin is Zycus's sharpest differentiation vs Coupa/Ariba's older AI and directly solves Brad's 'team of one' pain. These three moves—pilot to accelerate, multi-thread to secure, Merlin to differentiate—are the highest-leverage plays to convert this warm, high-fit early discovery into a win before the competitive field enters in 6-12 months.

competitive_position.summary: Six competitors or alternatives named by Brad Pittman on the 13 Apr intro call, all from his prior experience, NOT from an active Welltower evaluation (no RFP or shortlist yet). Brad explicitly criticized bolt-on integration pain with JAGGAER/Combinenet and Workday/Scout RFP and agreed that 'ARIBA and Coupa got a bit lost' in the same trap, validating Zycus's unified-platform wedge. When Brad launches evaluation in 6-12 months, Coupa and SAP Ariba will be the HIGHEST threats (default enterprise S2P choices, well-known, heavily marketed, strong in large multi-entity environments despite integration critiques). JAGGAER, Zip, Scout RFP, and Combinenet are LOWER threats (Brad's negative sentiment on integration and UX). The 'do nothing' / status quo (fragmented operating-partner procurement with optional industry aggregator) is a MEDIUM threat (Brad is mandate-driven but resource-constrained and 6-12 months out; internal prioritization or budget pressure could delay). The unnamed 'industry aggregator' Brad mentioned is an incumbent alternative but not detailed. Current state: no active competitive engagement; Zycus is positioned early and positively; competitive bake-off will begin when Brad re-engages in 6-12 months. How we win: move first with a quantified pilot (ANA or sourcing event) that builds Brad's business case; own the 'unified platform for multi-entity complexity' wedge Brad validated; activate Merlin autonomous sourcing agent (Brad showed strong interest: 'that could be really beneficial') to differentiate from Coupa/Ariba's older AI and from Zip/JAGGAER's narrower footprints.
